# Summative challenge: multi-factor Poisson with random effects

This brings the whole day together. You will build and fit a model that combines
**everything**: count data, a log link, multiple factors, and random effects for
grouping.

## The scenario

Fish **counts** were recorded across sites, under two experimental factors
(e.g. habitat and season), with multiple **reefs** sampled per condition. You
want the factor effects while accounting for reef-to-reef variation.

## The model

$$
\text{count}_i \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_i), \qquad
\log(\lambda_i) = \alpha + \beta_A A_i + \beta_B B_i + b_{\text{reef}[i]}, \qquad
b_j \sim \text{Normal}(0, \sigma_b)
$$

```
model {
  for (i in 1:N) {
    count[i] ~ dpois(lambda[i])
    log(lambda[i]) <- alpha + beta_a * A[i] + beta_b * B[i] + b[reef[i]]
  }
  for (j in 1:J) { b[j] ~ dnorm(0, sigma.b) }
  alpha   ~ dnorm(0, 10)
  beta_a  ~ dnorm(0, 5)
  beta_b  ~ dnorm(0, 5)
  sigma.b ~ dunif(0, 10)
}
```

## Bring your whole toolkit

- Set sensible, weakly informative priors (Module 19).
- **Run a prior predictive check** before fitting (Module 7).
- After fitting, check **R-hat and ESS** (Module 10) — reparameterise if needed.
- Run a **posterior predictive check** (Module 9) to confirm the model
  reproduces the counts.
- Interpret factor effects multiplicatively (Module 13).

## Your task

Fit the full model to `random-effects.csv` and check $\alpha$, $\beta_A$,
$\beta_B$, and the reef SD $\sigma_b$. Pass this and you have fit a real
hierarchical GLM end to end. 🎉
